what_it_does: This file is a prescriptive constitution for the voice/engines module: it defines the architecture, shared HTTP contract, port map, guarantees, and rules for wrapping multiple text-to-speech models as one service per voice.
observations: Six engines are named: kokoro (baseline) plus chatterbox, orpheus, cosyvoice, xtts, qwen3tts for the trial.
Each engine runs as its own HTTP service on a dedicated port (4123–4128).
All engines expose the same three endpoints: POST /tts, GET /voices, GET /health.
Model libraries must be imported lazily inside _engine(), never at module top.
Failures must be loud: 500 with a reason, never a silent/empty WAV.
A refined-Australian reference clip does not exist yet; clip-driven engines must read COMPANY_VOICE_REF and fail if absent.
Each engine gets its own venv because dependency pins conflict.
XTTS-v2 weights are flagged non-commercial and restricted to trial-by-ear only.
The file explicitly states none of the wrappers have been run yet and must be verified post-restart.
New engines are added by implementing synth, voices, optional warm/_engine, and calling _service.serve.
standouts: Service-per-voice architecture with a shared HTTP shell and per-engine ports/venvs.
Lazy, guarded heavy imports to prevent import-time crashes.
Explicit boundary honesty: wrappers are unverified and must be run before trusted.
XTTS non-commercial licensing guardrail embedded in the constitution.
conventions: Uses a constitution frontmatter with type, register, module, aliases, tags, relates-to, status.
Follows a prescriptive register style with What it is / What it guarantees / Where new things go / Its seam / Never / Boundary sections.
Mandates lazy imports and fail-loud error semantics.
Documents known-unknowns and verification gaps directly inside the file.
concerns: The constitution prescribes runtime behavior for wrappers that the author states have not yet been executed.
Heavy model dependencies are so conflicting that each engine needs a separate venv, increasing operational complexity.
A required reference clip (COMPANY_VOICE_REF) does not exist yet, blocking clip-driven engines until it is provided.
notes: A design contract for isolated TTS engine services that is explicit about what is prescribed versus what still needs to be run and verified.
questions: What is the exact implementation of voice/engines/_service.py and the per-engine <name>.py wrappers?
How are the per-engine venvs built, launched, and orchestrated?
What is the expected path/format/value of COMPANY_VOICE_REF?
How does voice/loop.py select an engine and map the voice argument for each engine?
What does the date 2026-06-03 refer to, given it is in the future relative to typical current dates?
How does the bridge proxy /api/tts route requests to the correct engine port?
purpose_vs_actual: Matches — the file states it is a constitution for voice/engines and then prescribes the engine layer's contract, guarantees, and constraints.
intention_signals: To create a uniform, extensible farm of isolated TTS engine services that share one runtime contract, can be added without coupling heavy dependencies, and fail loudly rather than silently, while being transparent that the wrappers remain unverified.
novelty: It treats an architectural rule set as a living constitution with a prescriptive register, and couples that with an explicit 'not yet run' boundary note and per-engine venv isolation for conflicting model dependencies.
contribution: A prescriptive contract and rule set for adding TTS engines, including the shared HTTP endpoint shape, port allocation, lazy import discipline, failure semantics, reference-clip requirement, and licensing guardrails.
summary_for_embedding: A prescriptive constitution for the voice/engines module that defines a per-voice HTTP TTS service architecture, shared contract, port map, lazy import and fail-loud guarantees, and rules for adding engines.
intention_for_embedding: To standardize how multiple third-party TTS models are wrapped as isolated, interchangeable HTTP services behind a single runtime contract.
